title: EUROPEAN EDITION: The Busted Knuckles Build Off.
summary:
Few manufacturers have embraced the custom motorcycle world quite like Royal Enfield, and the sales figures are booming!. The Indian marque has spent years actively investing in the people who keep motorcycle culture moving forward: builders, fabricators and independent workshops and The Busted Knuckles Build Off has become one of the clearest examples of that commitment. Returning to London's Bike Shed Show for 2026, the competition's European leg challenged six of the continent's best to transform the Shotgun 650 into something entirely their own. The only real rule was that there weren't many rules at all, and the results were every bit as diverse as you'd expect. 

The Shotgun 650 is almost purpose-built for this kind of challenge. Beneath its factory sheet metal lies a platform that's simple, honest and remarkably adaptable. That's a rare quality in today's motorcycle market, where electronics, integrated bodywork and increasingly complex engineering can make customisation feel like a battle against the manufacturer. Royal Enfield took the opposite approach, creating a machine that encourages experimentation. The results from this year's Build Off prove just how much room there is to move.

title: Suzuki GSX750 Inazuma by Jerem Motorcycles.
summary:
By the late 1990s, Suzuki was winding down its legendary oil-cooled era, but not before slipping one last treat into the lineup. The 750 Inazuma borrowed proven GSX-R mechanicals and wrapped them in a classic roadster package that looked as though it had wandered in from another decade. Twin shocks, a broad fuel tank and a torquey inline-four made it a capable all-rounder, but it arrived at a time when fully faired sports bikes were grabbing all the attention. As a result, the Inazuma became one of Hamamatsu's forgotten machines, but in the hands of Jerem Motorcycles, it's back to the future with a vengeance. 

These forgotten gems are so often the sort of motorcycle that catches the attention of French builder Jérémie Duchampt. Through Jerem Motorcycles, he's built a reputation for taking unlikely donors and refining them with a level of finish and renewed spark that makes them highly sought after. For this project, the brief was simple: retain the Inazuma's mechanical honesty while bringing every aspect of its chassis, performance and appearance into the modern era.

title: Spin Cycle: The Enduring Appeal of Café Racers.
summary:
Café racers were born from a necessity that doesn’t exist today—and yet, they remain perennially cool. Why?

The lore was written in the late 50s and early 60s. Although it was a golden age for motorcycling, the chasm between road bikes and race bikes was wide.

These days, you can watch Nicolo Bulega win a WSBK race on Sunday—then head out on Monday, buy a brand new Ducati Panigale V4 R, and feel like a hero on your next breakfast run. But back then, race bikes were the equivalent of today’s MotoGP machines; purpose-built for racing and beyond the reach of mere mortals. Homologation racing only went mainstream a couple of decades later, flooding the market with race-bred motorcycles that had lights and plates.

You could buy a fast bike in the 50s and 60s, at least by the era’s standards. But pure racing metal, like the Norton Manx, was only available to official race teams and well-heeled privateers. Meanwhile, the MV Agusta Tre and other models of its ilk were downright unobtainable.

title: FIRST CLASS: Triumph Bonneville built for a Frenchman.
summary:
The modern Triumph Bonneville has become one of the most heavily customised motorcycles on the planet, but very few builders have explored the platform as extensively as Tamarit Motorcycles. With more than 200 Triumph-based projects now completed from their Spanish workshop, they've developed a knack for producing bikes that are instantly recognisable as Tamarit creations without simply repeating the same formula. Build number 153, aptly named First, is a perfect example, built for a Frenchman who wanted more than a little Brat personality in his T120. 

'First' was commissioned by French owner Patrick, who wanted something personal rather than a catalogue exercise or a carbon copy style custom. He also wanted to work with a builder who would take on his ideas and genuinely reflect them back in what they built. So, while the donor bike retains the robust parallel twin and modern electronics that make the T120 such a capable foundation, almost everything surrounding the engine has been reconsidered. The result is a machine that combines brat-style proportions with enough café racer influence to keep things looking purposeful rather than fashionable.

title: THE RELOAD: Royal Enfield Shotgun by RSD.
summary:
There are few motorcycles on the market right now proving themselves as adaptable as Royal Enfield's 650 twin. In the space of a few short years, we've seen the platform transformed into choppers, desert sleds, café racers and land speed weapons. But Roland Sands Design has taken things in a different direction. Their latest collaboration with Royal Enfield, the Reload 650, turns the Shotgun cruiser into something that feels lifted straight from the glory days of AMA Superbike racing. Unveiled at Portland's One Motorcycle Show, it's a machine that blends vintage race-bike attitude with the kind of thoughtful engineering that has become RSD's trademark.

What makes the Reload so compelling is that it doesn't rely on excess. There are no oversized fairings, wild graphics or gimmicks fighting for attention. Instead, the entire design pivots around a single idea. "I wanted to give it kind of like an 80s retro superbike vibe," Roland explains. That vision started at the rear of the motorcycle, where a completely redesigned tail section stretches the bike's proportions and transforms its silhouette. The stock Shotgun's relaxed cruiser stance disappears, replaced by something leaner, longer and considerably more athletic.

title: CYCLONE: GSX-R1000R by HAXCH MOTO.
summary:
The late 1980s and early 1990s were golden years for Suzuki. From the endurance-racing influence of the GSX-R range to the razor-sharp RGV250, Hamamatsu produced some of the most recognisable sportbikes ever built. For Marc Bell of HAXCH MOTO, those machines provided the starting point for this very machine, Cyclone, a limited-production reimagining of the GSX-R1000R that combines Suzuki's most celebrated design language with the performance of its latest superbike platform. Built around the current L7 GSX-R1000R platform, the Cyclone is effectively a current-generation superbike wearing an entirely new skin. Breathtakingly beautiful, and blisteringly fast in equal measures.

HAXCH is a very well-known name in the custom scene, and has earned a first-class reputation for combining modern engineering tools with a deep appreciation for classic Japanese performance motorcycles. Bell's objective with Cyclone, however, wasn't to recreate a single model, but to distil the visual DNA of Suzuki's greatest race replicas into one cohesive package. "The Cyclone represents exactly what we stand for," he explains. "We wanted to build a bike that gives you the visual nostalgia of the 80s and 90s, but with the blisteringly fast, razor-sharp dynamics of a 2026 superbike."

title: Classy Nuisance: Yamaha Virago Cafe Racer.
summary:
When most people talk about starting from scratch, they're usually exaggerating. Rusten Smith had no such luxury. His 1995 Yamaha Virago 535 arrived completely disassembled and packed into boxes, looking more like the aftermath of a garage clean-out than the beginning of a custom motorcycle. But where others might have seen a headache, Rusten saw opportunity. Having always wanted to build a cafe racer of his own, he decided the forgotten V-twin would become the perfect canvas for a first full-scale custom project. One year later, the result is 'Classy Nuisance', a beautiful hand-built machine that sheds 30kg from the stock bike and bears little resemblance to the Yamaha that donated its VIN plate.

Viragos have long enjoyed a second life in the custom world. Yamaha's air-cooled V-twin platform is reliable, affordable and mechanically straightforward, making it a favourite among garage builders looking to create something unique without breaking the bank. Most projects focus on simplifying the factory package, but Rusten's ambitions went much further. Rather than modify the existing chassis, which is awful, he placed the original frame in a jig, retained the steering neck to preserve the bike's identity and began designing an entirely new structure around it.

title: Bangkok Hotrod Custom Show 2026.
summary:
You hear whispers of the wild custom scene in Asia, people who go and visit rave about the unique style and creativity on display in these communities around the world. Until recently I was one of the people running off second hand information and snippets I had seen on the internet. So I decided to go and check it out for myself. The destination Bangkok Hotrod Custom Show 2026.

This show hosts every facet of custom culture to be put in one place and celebrated in the beating heart of Thailand. Custom Motorcycles and Cars are prominent, but supporting this are showcases of Pin striping, music, fashion, machinery, custom tuk-tuks and everything in between. As we weaved through the show hall the first thing that struck me was the variation in these bikes on display. After a while you cannot be surprised by what engine a builder has made into what style of bike. A 60’s Kawasaki into a drag bike, or a 70cc Japanese 2 stroke built into a perfectly proportioned (just scaled down) 60’s chopper.

title: ROEDPILEN: Husqvarna Vitpilen 701 by XTR Pepo.
summary:
There are custom motorcycles that borrow from endurance racing, and then there are machines built by Pepo Rosell. For more than two decades, the man behind XTR Pepo has been refining a style that now influences custom workshops across the globe: track style bodywork, exposed mechanicals, race-bred ergonomics and a visual aggression that makes factory bikes look ornamental by comparison. His latest creation, the “Roedpilen”, takes that philosophy in an entirely new direction, transforming a 2018 Husqvarna Vitpilen 701 into a razor-edged endurance racer that looks ready to line up at the Bol d’Or.

The donor platform alone makes this build fascinating. Pepo’s name is often tied to Ducati-powered customs, dating back to his groundbreaking Radical Ducati era, but the compact single-cylinder Husqvarna gives the Roedpilen a very different personality. The Vitpilen’s lightweight chassis and narrow engine architecture allow the entire motorcycle to shrink-wrap itself around the mechanical package. The result is visually closer to a late-70s Formula racer than a modern street bike, with an impossibly slim waistline, minimal frontal area and proportions that look completely functional rather than styled.

title: SMOKE MACHINE: Yamaha RD350 from Spain.
summary:
Some motorcycles earn respect through the sheer chaos that is riding them and gain a mythology that to this day fills people with fear. The Yamaha RD350 is one such machine. By the time the final iteration rolled off the factory floor, it was loud, light, twitchy and unapologetically two-stroke at a time when manufacturers were still figuring out how much insanity they could legally sell to the public. Decades later, the RD still carries that same reputation, equal parts street racer, smoke machine and giant killer, and now Lord Drake Kustoms has dragged one screaming into the modern era.

When Yamaha unleashed the RD350 in the 1970s, it changed the formula overnight. Here was a middleweight twin that weighed next to nothing, revved like it had something to prove, and delivered power with the subtlety of a brick through a shop window. Riders loved them because they felt alive. Dealers loved them because they sold fast. Tyre companies probably loved them because owners kept accidentally turning rear rubber into smoke. The RD became the bike that taught an entire generation about expansion chambers, power bands, and the fine art of hanging on. Sure, there were other smokers at the time, but few truly had such a lasting impact.

title: FULLY LOADED: 1000cc Royal Enfield Shotgun.
summary:
Winning the Busted Knuckles Build Off takes more than simply building a good custom motorcycle. It demands a machine capable of stopping people mid-scroll, silencing a room in person and pushing far enough beyond expectations that it redefines the platform beneath it. That is exactly what Dylan Brown achieved with the RE1000. The bike marks build number one under the Brawldonny Metalworks banner, operating out of the enormous Gold Coast dealership, Grid Motorcycle.

Starting with the Royal Enfield Shotgun 650 platform, Brown transformed the bike into something far more radical: a fully hand-fabricated custom centred around a world-first 1000cc parallel twin. Drawing heavily from Japanese custom philosophy and old-school fabrication techniques, the RE1000 feels less like a dealer-built project and more like a hand-built concept machine that escaped the floor of the Yokohama Hot Rod Custom Show.

title: Creative Prowess: Rough Crafts Delivers a Vivid Harley Chopper.
summary:
In the world of AI slop, the prompt is king. The more instructions you feed any given AI model, the better your chances of achieving a somewhat passable result. And this is why AI will never replace the likes of Winston Yeh. As the creative force behind the legendary Taiwanese custom house Rough Crafts, Winston is accustomed to working with briefs that are virtually non-existent. Most of his clients simply ask for a build in his signature style, add one or two vague preferences, and leave it at that.

It’s unsurprising, given his portfolio. Often imitated, but never emulated, the Rough Crafts aesthetic sits at the intersection of brutality and luxury, melding monochromatic finishes and aggressive proportions with high-end finishes and top-shelf components. True to form, Winston’s latest client had very little to offer in the way of creative direction. With one murdered-out Rough Crafts build already in his garage, he asked for something in a lighter hue and mentioned that he liked choppers. Winston responded with a striking Harley chopper that’s loaded with slick details and atypical touches.

title: The Gringo’s Tales Book Review
summary:
Published in: Media

The Gringo’s Tales: Two friends, two motorcycles, and over 9,000 miles across a wild, untamed Mexico

I’d like to introduce you to a motorcycle travel book that had me smiling and almost horrified from the start. Only ‘almost’ because actually, it reminded me of how I travelled when I was younger. As in, have the idea and then spend my time focusing on making a trip happen, rather than on how inadequately prepared and underfunded I was.

Michele Ricucci is a very well-known and respected photographer and drone pilot. He has had photo exhibitions in multiple places around the world and the footage he gathers is simply fantastic. ‘The Gringo’s Tale’ is his first attempt at writing a book, and let me start my comments by saying that I hope it’s not the last.

Good photographers, to my mind, create pictures that tell a story with a magic blend of either subtlety or bluntness, drama and beauty. The very best seem to weave in an element of ‘clever’ that they see and few others do, until the photographer shows it to them. When I first heard of this book I wondered if Michele could create similarly impactful pictures with words. He can.

'The Gringo’s Tales' takes you riding over 9,000 miles across Mexico. Michele sets off with Roberto, a restauranteur friend from Italy, on two decidedly dodgy bikes. Bikes that ‘everyone’ was telling them not to ride across Mexico on. The point was that their budgets were tiny; that saying about tailors cutting their suits according to the cloth they have, came straight into my mind.

Michele is a man who when he made this trip had already bought a second-hand Honda XR 150 in South America and explored on it for over 21,000 miles. Oh, and I should mention that he didn’t have a bike license…

There are a series of layers to the book which kept my attention more than held. One of those is that he has a desire to see the world away from booking dot.com and tourist sites. He’s driven to explore off the beaten track and to meet, to spend time with people who have no choice but to make life work with courage and ingenuity, the hard way, but yet have kindness and appreciation firmly in the front of their minds.

Michele is a man who spends less time worrying about how clean a place is but focuses on the point that there is a place to sleep. He’s a man who listens to advice, and then makes a plan to follow his instincts. Most of the time his instincts are both courageous, and spot on. At times they had me asking if he had all his marbles. But read on respectfully and full of hope.

Other than saying that his 'word pictures' are great!, I really don’t want to give more away about this book, or will detract from the raw magnetism.

What I do want to say is that if you ride a motorcycle, you will be interested in this book. If you love to travel by motorcycle, you’ll be in awe, and yep perhaps horrified in sections. At others, you’ll be blown away by the stories – the tales of the unexpected, and the skill of the storytelling. I suspect you’ll end the book thinking , ‘Wow, what a ride.’

I had to have a long moment’s silence to let what I’d been reading settle in my mind. It still hasn’t. For sure it’s a keeper and I have no doubt that I’ll be reading it several times more.

Published in January 2026, 'The Gringo’s Tales' is available from Amazon.

Sam Manicom spent eight years traveling around the world by motorcycle and is the author of four motorcycle travel books. All are available from Amazon in both paperback and Kindle. His first book, Into Africa, is now downloadable from iTunes as an enhanced eBook. Sam-Manicom.com

title: End of an Era: Wolfman Luggage Closes After 34 Years
summary:
Published in: Industry

Wolfman Luggage, a beloved name in adventure and dual‑sport motorcycle gear, is officially closing its doors as Eric Hougen, its longtime owner and founder, retires. After more than three decades of creating durable, rider‑focused soft luggage, the company’s shutdown marks the end of an era for riders who trusted Wolfman panniers, tank bags, and duffels on countless journeys. And, I'm one of them! 

When I started riding over 30 years ago, Wolfman Luggage was one of the top options on the market. For years, I used a Wolfman magnetic tank bag which not only shared my adventure around North America on a Bandit 1200, but then followed me to explore the wild and largely unknown Western regions of China. For me, it was sometimes storage, sometimes pillow, but always a reliable companion.

Then later, after taking over ADVMoto in 2010, I had a chance to work with Eric. His passion for the sport, dedication to community and willingness to roll up his sleeves to be involved in every step of the work was, and is, always inspirational.

Wolfman Luggage was founded and operated in Colorado but he's since moved to a place where where he plans to: "Ride, ride, hike, bike and forget what day it is." While it's sad to see a long time brand icon leave the industry, I'm happy he's made a decision to move on and enjoy life to the fullest, having left a hugely positive wake of impact in his community.   

For those unfamiliar with the brand, let's take a quick personal look at the history of Wolfman Luggage  and what this means for adventure motorcyclists who have relied on its gear for years.

Wolfman Motorcycle Luggage: A Brief History

Like many great brands in the motorcycle world,  the “company” started simple in 1992. Eric used to work with Mountainsmith bags of Golden, Colorado, another outdoor luggage brand who started small to make high-quality backpacks for serious outdoorsmen. You can see this influence in the badges used on both brands.

Back in the day, many companies were basically a sewing machine, a pile of fabric, and a stubborn belief that bags could be tougher, smarter, and easier to live with. Before long, Wolfman bags were showing up on bikes that were going a lot farther than the local dirt trails. The designs were simple, useful and evolved in features and quality over time.  Moreover, they had a handmade feel you only get when the person stitching the seams actually rides.

As adventure and dual‑sport riding took off, Wolfman grew alongside it. You’d see the gear pop up on forums, in ride reports, and at rallies often scuffed, dusty and sun‑faded, but still hanging on after countless days of exposure and bad decisions. The brand didn’t brag about the quality of their product, it just kept showing up in the background of riders' best stories.

Over time, the line expanded from a few core bags into a whole ecosystem: tank bags, tail bags, duffels, dry bags, little pouches for tools and such. The look stayed familiar. It was functional and no‑nonsense, often in that recognizable black‑and‑yellow color sceme—but each new version felt a bit more refined. You got the sense that every stitch and strap had been argued over on some rocky trail long before it was ever strapped to a customers' bike.

What really set Wolfman apart, though, was the relationship with the riders. It felt less like a big “brand” and more like a small crew of people who genuinely cared if your gear made it through your next trip.  Riders wrote in with ideas, complaints, and crazy stories, and you could see that feedback show up in the next round of bags. It was a conversation, not a one‑way broadcast.

Moreover, Eric struggled, probably more than anyone I know in his segment, to make his bags in the USA. For many years, Wolfman Motorcycle Luggage built its soft bags domestically and leaned into “hand made in America” as part of its identity. As the company grew and introduced its RF‑welded WP (waterproof) line, production for those welded products moved overseas (to China) in order to stay price competitive.

While design and testing remained in the USA, Wolfman later launched “Wolfman Threadworks” and PACK bicycle bags as new, made‑to‑order, US‑made sewn lines, bringing a portion of manufacturing back stateside.

So when news started to spread that Wolfman Luggage would be closing its doors as the owner stepped into retirement, it hit a little different. It wasn’t just, “Oh, another company is gone.” It was more like hearing an old riding buddy was parking the bike for good.

Wolfman riders, including myself, don’t just remember the logo—we remember the miles logged with these bags strapped to the bike, the trips that might not have happened without gear they trusted. Wolfman’s story isn’t just about luggage; it’s about a small, rider‑driven outfit that quietly shaped how a lot of us pack for adventure.

We'd like to wish Eric the best of times in retirement and hope to catch him on the trail one day soon. Until then, I'd like to encourage everyone to support private and family owned business in the community.  As many industries have shrunk, now dominated by international brands, the heartbeat of what made passion-based, community driven, American innovation is slowly disappearing.

Although some economic forces can not be controlled, liking, sharing or taking a moment to tell someone you appreciate their work and letting them know how it impacts your life is FREE!

Images from Carl Parker and @wolfmanluggage Instagram page.

title: 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 Review
summary:
Published in: Bikes

Is the 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 right for you?

Verdict

The 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 is a lively, mid-size adventure twin that shines on pavement and fast gravel, offering more power and excitement than most of its direct rivals while staying approachable for a wide range of riders. It falls short for hardcore off-road or long-distance luggage-heavy travel, but as a do-it-all commuter, weekend tourer, and light ADV explorer, it hits a very sweet spot.

Kawasaki revived the KLE name with the 2026 Kawasaki KLE500, a modern 500‑class adventure twin that delivers class‑leading power, engaging street performance, and enough off‑road capability to finally tackle those gravel roads you usually ride past. Slotting into the booming mid-size adventure motorcycle segment, it targets riders who want one bike to commute during the week, tour on weekends, and still have funt when the pavement ends. To see what this new adventure machine is capable of, we rode the KLE500 in Utah for sweeping curves, deep sand, and relentless headwinds. 

Slotting neatly between small‑capacity singles and big‑bore ADV behemoths, the KLE500 aims to be the one bike that can commute during the week, munch miles on the weekend, and still feel at home when the pavement ends. It’s entering a seriously crowded space—up against bikes like the KTM 390 Adventure R, CFMOTO Ibex 450, Moto Morini Vettore 450, and Royal Enfield Himalayan 450—but Kawasaki is clearly betting that a fun engine, sensible ergonomics, attractive price tag, and a recognizable badge will win over a lot of riders. Is it enough to dominate the arena?

• Some History: Bringing Back The KLE Name

The KLE badge has been around longer than many riders realize. Kawasaki first used it in the early 1990s on the original KLE500, a twin-cylinder all-rounder sold mainly in Europe which used the venerable Ninja 500 motor we came to love in North America. That bike was part commuter, part light tourer and part dual-sport which made it a practical, slightly quirky machine. Although it was criticized for being underpowered with weak brakes, the old KLE never made huge headlines, but it earned respect from riders who prioritized comfort and versatility more than outright performance.

When the first generation KLE500 disappeared from the lineup in the 2007, Kawasaki’s adventure duties split between the road-focused Versys (called the KLE 650) series and the single-cylinder KLR 650. Despite it's demise, some of the KLE500's fairing styling lived on with the long overdue KLR 650 redesign in 2008.

That left a gap: there was no mid-size, twin-cylinder adventure model that followed the KLE affordable, fun, do-it-all formula. Fast forward to 2026 and that gap is finally filled. The new KLE500 is a modern reboot that borrows Kawasaki’s latest 451 cc parallel-twin platform and wraps it in a package clearly designed for mixed-surface riding. Where the original KLE was ahead of its time, the new model arrives right as demand for affordable and manageable ADVs is rising.

• What’s New on the 2026 KLE500: Engine, Chassis And Key Features

At the heart of the new KLE500 is Kawasaki’s 451 cc liquid-cooled parallel twin, shared with the Ninja 500, but is tuned for broader midrange while keeping that screaming top-end output of 51hp from the factory. The numbers won’t scare anyone at around 51 hp and 31.7 lb-ft of torque, but it's still about 10hp more than it's nearest class competitor. Plus, it delivers that power in a fairly linear manner all the way up to it's 10,000RPM redline. It’s smooth, eager to rev, and most importantly, very predictable which makes this an incredibly fun bike on asphalt.  Even better, thanks to the Ninja 500's success, there's already good aftermarket analysis and support to make notable improvements in the new KLE's output, that will honestly bury it's modern competition in terms of output.

For riders coming from sport or naked bikes, the engine feels familiar. It likes to spin up and doesn’t surprise you with a sudden torque spike that can send you sideways on loose surfaces. As a lower speed rider, it's easy to miss the every-day friendly mid-range torque as it does require riders to keep it higher in the rev range. That said, it will lug out of low-rpm, missed-shift turns without much complaint, but this is definitely a top-end biased engine more oriented towards "spirited riding."  I found it fun and exciting to have a motor to push hard all day, but doesn't have so much power I worry about breaking traction or speed limits too quickly. Can the 451cc motor take it? For sure, it's bred for this and is accompanied by a healthy sized radiator to manage the heat.

For riders stepping down from bigger bikes, it offers enough power to make highway passes and fast backroad runs enjoyable, without the “I’m one mistake away from disaster” feeling some big twins carry in the dirt. We were able to pull up to 70mph in 3rd gear going up hill into the wind on an entrance ramp. This leaves plenty of room for relaxed highway cruising thanks to the six-speed gearbox with a slipper clutch which provides a light lever pull and tidies up aggressive downshifts. That’s a comfort bonus in traffic and a confidence booster on loose descents or when you misjudge a corner and bang down a gear too many.

Regarding vibrations, I personally felt it was very smooth all the way through according to my butt, feet and hands "vibrometer." I also use mirror vibration to gauge this, and there were very little vibrations through the KLE's stylish, but small mirrors. Over the past 20 years of moto-journalism, I've found every rider is sensitive to vibrations in their own way so while I'd say it wasn't bad, it's normal to have different opinions on this and encourage riders to get some first-hand experience on the bike.

On the chassis side, Kawasaki has made all the right moves for credibility in the adventure space beginning with a new, rigid trellis frame that weighs only 40 lbs of it's 430lb wet weight. The overall build quality of the bike is solid and simple.

All of this rolls on a 21-inch front and 17-inch rear spoked wheel combo, shod with IRC GP-410 Trail Winner ADV-oriented tires. While the IRC tires were OK and no testers had any major problems during the test, I think upgrading the stock tries with your favorite set of skins would be one of my first upgrades.

One picking point of the community is the 17" rear wheel with a slightly odd 140/70-17 size. It does make the rear wheel a little "small" for  dual-sport applications, but for most riders who plane to spend a vast majority of their time on smoother surfaces, its not a problem. Actually, there's one benefit to it, which is it help keep the saddle height down to a relatively low 34". I was able to completely flatfoot the new KLE500 with a 32" inseam also partly thanks to its narrow tank profile.  While you could remedy this by installing a higher profile tire, or 18" rim, we don't think it will be necessary for most riders except that moving to a 140/80-17 gets you a much wider selection of adventure tires. 

This leads us to another hot topic of the new KLE having "only" 7.3 inches of ground clearance which was largely overblown by the internet. Our testing route took us through some sandy single track with plenty of ruts whoops and washouts. For the most part, the clearance wasn't a problem except for the most aggressive riders who like to catch air. The real off-road limitation on the KLE500 isn't so much the ground clearance, but the suspension, which we'll talk about next.

Suspension is handled by a 43 mm upside-down cartridge fork up front and a preload-adjustable shock at the rear, with enough travel (~8 inches front and rear) to take on rough backroads and moderate double-track without too much drama. While many riders in the group were perfectly able to handle some of the more difficult single track at speed, the stock, largely unadjustable suspension is by-far the 2026 model's biggest hinderance in terms of off-road performance.

Fuel capacity sits at about 4.2 gallons, which puts real-world range in the “a couple hundred miles if you’re sensible” neighborhood. We were seeing over 50mpg with aggressive riding in windy conditions, so expect mid 50's under normal riding. It’s not a huge tank, but for most riders mixing commuting, day rides, and occasional multi-day trips, it's more than adequate.

• Ergonomics And Equipment

The standard KLE500 ABS offers the same engine and suspension as the higher-spec KLE500 SE ABS for an additional $900. The SE adds a 4.3-inch color TFT display with smartphone connectivity, a taller touring screen, sturdier metal reinforced handguards, a more substantial skid plate, and full LED lighting touches that many riders would end up adding aftermarket anyway. From a “live with it every day” standpoint, that SE package makes a lot of sense. Newer riders get clearer information and modern connectivity without the fuss, while experienced riders get practical protection and comforts baked in from the factory. 

The cockpit is where the KLE500 really sells itself to both newcomers and downsizers. I found the riding position neutral and upright, with a relaxed reach to the bars and enough legroom for most riders to stay comfortable all day, even in the stock seat. Fun feature is the two piece seat comes with a replaceable rear section of various heights, so it's possible to get a more "bench" like seat instead of the stock stepped design. This would be an important upgrade to allow bigger riders to slide further back in the saddle.

The tank and mid-section of the bike is relatively narrow, which makes it easier for shorter riders to get a foot down despite the adventure-ready seat height. For a 6-foot rider, the pegs felt a little high, but I think removing the included rubber pad and/or getting lowering pegs/adapters would reduce the knee angle for those concerned.

Last, but not least, are the host of features like a bright and easily readable color screen with all-round LED lighting (on SE models). Unfortunately, we didn't get a chance to test the headlights at night. The TFT display features smartphone connectivity via Kawasaki's Rideology app which allows you to see some bike bike data on the phone, and display some info, like navigation instructions, on your display.

• On-Road Performance: Fun, Friendly and Familiar

On pavement, the KLE500 is a bike that invites you to relax a little without ever feeling dull. The parallel twin pulls cleanly from low revs and wakes up nicely in the top end giving you enough punch to navigate through traffic or pull out of corners with a smile. It’s not trying to be a mini superbike – and that is exactly why it works so well as an everyday machine. Riders coming from sport or street backgrounds will find the transition surprisingly easy. You still get that satisfaction of letting the engine spin and timing your upshifts just right, but the chassis underneath you is more forgiving.

Adventure bikes make great touring and commuter bikes. The long-travel suspension smooths out potholes and rough road patches, and the 21-inch front wheel shrugs off the kind of broken surfaces that would rattle a pure street bike. Stock suspension settings feel like they are dialed for pavement surfing as the low speed damping feels firm which makes for great fun on asphalt. At highway speeds, the KLE500 turns and tracks predictably with no surprises.

The twin hums along without feeling frantic, and while it will never be as effortless as a 1200, it also doesn’t come with the cost, weight, bulk and wear of those bigger machines. For downsizing riders, the first impression will be: “This is lighter and easier everywhere, but I can still sit at 70–75 mph without feeling like I’m wringing its neck.” I was able to pull 75mph indicated at 11k rpm in 3rd gear with peak horsepower right at the redline. This produces 10hp more than it's nearest competitor and is where the KLE500 sets itself apart. With three speeds left to go, there's plenty of room to cruise at 80 and some left over for passing. 

Wind protection is solid for a bike in this class although I found the tall fairing provided some helmet buffeting above 60mph which was amplified by the helmets visor. The windscreen can be adjusted, but it is not tool-less so take a few minutes to setup.

Braking performance is sensible more than sensational but I found it nicely balanced for both street and off-road use. It's firm but not overly grabby, or too soft. A single 300mm disc up front and a single 230mm rear, provide steady, predictable stopping without overwhelming initial bite. That’s exactly what you want on a bike that spends time on loose surfaces as well as asphalt – enough power to stop you quickly on road, without the kind of grabby response that can spell trouble on gravel.

It's also worth mentioning for the motorheads who like powermax smaller engines, the KLE500 may be your ride. Many years of tuners working on the Ninja 500 means there are ready upgrades and mappings which would take the KLE500's output and response to the next level with minimal mods.  We'll feature separate stories on KLE500 power upgrades down the road.

• Off-Road Performance and Limitations

Take the KLE500 off the pavement and its design priorities become very clear. This is not a hardcore rally weapon, despite it's distinctive Gundam-esque rally looks and accompanying slogan "Life's a Rally." Instead, it’s built to make off-road riding accessible and enjoyable for riders who might be new to dirt, or simply tired of wrestling a 600-plus-pound giant around on loose surfaces.

The 21-inch front wheel is a big part of that. It rolls over ruts, rocks, and small obstacles much more willingly than the 19-inch setups common on more road-biased “ADV-style” bikes. At worst, the stock suspension settings felt a little harsh on hard washboard and the fork bottomed out twice on some deep whoops. If you keep the speeds down, the bike and suspension track nicely off-road and should provide enough confidence for new riders to get their dirt chops.

You can stand up, look ahead, and let the bike move around beneath you without panicking at every wiggle. To reduce forward lean and neck angles, riders over 6 feet tall, some may want to add a bar riser and remove the rubber pad on the pegs.

Another great off-road oriented feature is it comes with a very simple system to turn off the ABS. Press and hold the giant red "No ABS" button on the left side switch gear turns off both front and rear wheel ABS. I really appreciate how easy this is to do without having to hit small dash buttons or perform a magic "up-up-down-down" sequence reserved for getting 30 lives at Contra.  

The engine’s smooth powerband and light clutch pull helps a little here. Because the twin builds power progressively rather than hitting all at once, it’s much easier to manage traction with small throttle adjustments, even if your off-road technique is still a work in progress. I'd still prefer a more torque in the lower RPM range for dedicated off-road use, but for a bike that excels on the highway, the setup is perfectly fine for riders new to dirt.

Awkward U-turns on uneven ground or picking up a dropped bike, all of these tasks become significantly less intimidating when the bike weighs a hundred pounds less and your feet can touch the ground. I got personal experience on picking up the KLE500. Coming down a rutted, off-camber sandy track at low speed, the rear wheel went left and we fell over to the right. The KLE500 was super easy to pick up with only minimal scuffing on the upper fairing, The design protected important internals like the radiator. I'm guessing a tip-over sensor was activated and needed to be reset. After power cycling the ignition, she started right up and I was on my way.  

Push the KLE500 hard through big whoops, sand, fast single-tracks and its middleweight nature and comfort-focused suspension tuning will eventually remind you this is an all-rounder, not an off-road race bike. But that’s not really its mission. Its sweet spot is 60–80% of your off-road pace, where you’re exploring, making progress, and having fun rather than trying to set stage times.

There's lots to love about the bike, and we expect that future generations of the KLE500 will add more tunable off-road ready suspension. They are introducing a brand new model in a very competitive segment and I believe its to keep the initial price down to market test the model, while giving some room for later updates and revisions. Everything else is there, from the build quality, power, dealer support, braking, geometry and ergonomics but I can't wait to see what aftermarket options become available and how this will dramatically impact its off-road potential.

• KLE500 Versus the Competition

The KLE500 doesn’t exist in a vacuum. It’s stepping into a fiercely competitive part of the market, with direct rivals like the KTM 390 Adventure R, CFMOTO Ibex 450, Moto Morini Vettore 450, and Royal Enfield Himalayan 450 all vying for attention. Each of these bikes has its own personality, and which one is “best” depends heavily on what fits your riding needs.

The KTM 390 Adventure R is arguably the sharpest tool in the box: lighter (by about 40-50lbs), more aggressive, and loaded with electronics. Its single-cylinder engine is lively and high-strung, great for sporty riders who enjoy a busy, tech-packed experience. The trade-off is a more nervous feel off-road and a buzzier feel on longer highway stints. The Royal Enfield Himalayan 450 is arguably closest in spirit to the KLE500. It’s friendly, torquey down low, with very balanced suspension, but it’s slower and more relaxed on the road. Riders who value plodding reliability and classic charm will love it; riders coming off sportbikes may find it a bigger step down in performance than they’d like.

The CFMOTO Ibex 450 (full review here) and Moto Morini Vettore 450 share a similar platform and lean heavily into value and feature lists. They offer strong spec-for-price value and are rapidly improving in reputation. Some key features these models have but are not found on the KLE500 are adjustable suspension, 18" rear wheel, an exgtra inch of clearance and tubeless rims. But in many markets they still can’t match Kawasaki’s dealer network, long-term parts support and, possibly, resale value.

The KLE500’s niche is clear: it’s the smooth, peppy twin that feels natural to street and sport riders, wrapped in an adventure package that looks good and doesn’t overwhelm newcomers or downsizers. It won’t out-tech the KTM or undercut the CFMOTO on price, but it delivers a polished, well-balanced experience backed by a brand and dealer network many riders already trust. 

• Final Thoughts: Who Sould Buy the KLE500

The KLE500 is a bike with a very clear audience in mind, and if you fit into either of these groups, it should be on your test-ride list. First, it’s a natural fit for new riders coming from sport or street backgrounds. If you’ve owned a streetbike and are now curious about gravel roads, BDR routes, or light adventure travel, the KLE500 is an easy choice. The engine speaks a language you already know, while the chassis gently introduces you to the looser, more forgiving rhythm of mixed surface riding. Whether you want to tour, commute or explore rough roads, the KLE500 has plenty to offer.

Second, it’s an excellent option for experienced riders looking to downsize from bigger, heavier ADV or touring rigs and want to stick with a familiar brand. Maybe your current bike is starting to feel like too much in the garage, in the parking lot, or on the trail – but you’re not ready to give up twin-cylinder smoothness or the ability to cover highway miles at a decent clip. The KLE500 lets you shed weight and complexity while keeping enough performance to still enjoy the ride. In some ways, it's like a mini-Tenere 700 without the foot-stomping, tire romping torque.

Don't let the initial social media, doom scrolling, echo chamber pundits get you down about the new KLE500. It's a great bike for the right rider. I think what we have is an incongruency between how the bike is presented and what the bike is meant to be by design. This is not a new problem and it's obvious to the community. Making the KLE500 look like a rally bike, then using the word "Rally" in the tagline messaging would naturally make someone think this is an aggressive, off road, race-ready bike—which it's not, at least in current stock form. I think it can be a serious off-road contender, and maybe one of the best in class, with aftermarket upgrades to suspension and tires. 

There's plenty of options on the market for taller enduro and dual-sport bikes. We wear different shoes for different occasions and bikes are no different. As such, we want to evaluate bikes for what they are intended to be according to price, features and application. This is about having options in the market to grow the community, not insisting every bike should satisfy all our demands, and be affordable to boot.

For the Kawasaki "Team Green" fans, we expect more than a few die-hard KLR650 riders will check out the KLE500 as a modern lightweight replacement. It would be an upgrade and replacement for the Versys 300, which is a bike we love, but was unfortunately not promoted much after the initial launch. The KLE500 may ultimately replace these aging platforms, and with some thoughtful future upgrades, be a top mid-sized ADV, do-it-all bike for a wide range to real-world riders. 

Pricing will vary by market, but Kawasaki positions the KLE500 competitively in the mid-size adventure space. The standard KLE500 ABS comes in at a price that undercuts many European rivals while still offering a modern engine and a practical spec sheet. In the USA it comes in three colorways: Metallic Carbon Gray/Ebony for the base model or Metallic Bluish Green and Pearl Blizzard White for the SE version. The SE ABS version costs $900 more but bundles in upgrades that most riders would likely add anyway, making it arguably the better value if your budget allows.

The final value proposition is simple: a smooth, flexible and forgiving twin that can do anything most riders would ask of it. The 2026 Kawasaki KLE500, and many of its midsize competitors, are not about bragging rights. It’s about having a bike you actually want to ride and be part of your daily life. Whether that's going to work, the store, or that gravel backroad you've had to cruise by too many times the new KLE is a great option. For new riders stepping into adventure and current riders sizing down from the big stuff, that might be the most compelling spec of all.

We're excited to see the new KLE500 fill out what's an amazing selection of mid-size adventure bikes, never before seen in North America, if not he world. There's never been a better time to get into riding, not only for the adventure, but as a companion you can enjoy everyday. No matter what you want to do, Kawasaki's new KLE500 can take you there and back again in a sharp-looking, fun, friendly and affordable package. You just have to get on, and ride!

For more information, visit your local Kawasaki dealer or visit: Kawasaki.com

Photos by: KawasakiUSA/Justin Coffey and Carl Parker

• Pros

  • Fun, high output, bulletproof engine with slipper clutch
  • Solid build quality with rigid frame
  • Comfortable seat height and off-road ergonomics
  • Edgy rally styling and riding geometry
  • Easy to disable ABS

 • Cons

  • Unfamiliar stock tires and odd rear tire size
  • Suspension tuned more for on-road riding than off-road
  • Non-adjustable suspension
  • Ground clearance limited for agressive or tecnical riding

 

Carl Parker is the publisher of ADVMoto (Adventure Motorcycle Magazine), where he has helped grow a niche adventure/dual‑sport title into an internationally distributed print and digital brand over the past 16 years.  A lifelong photographer, artist, rider and storyteller, Carl is known for championing small‑displacement adventures and expanding the ADV community through accessible, real‑world travel content for everyday people.

 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 – Key Specs

  • Engine: 451 cc liquid‑cooled DOHC parallel twin, 8 valves

  • Power: 51 hp @ 10,000 rpm

  • Torque: 31.7 lb‑ft @ 7,500 rpm

  • Transmission: 6‑speed, assist & slipper clutch, chain final drive

  • Frame: Trellis, high‑tensile steel

  • Front suspension: 43 mm inverted fork, approx. 8.3 in (210 mm) travel

  • Rear suspension: Uni‑Trak monoshock, preload‑adjustable, approx. 7.7 in (200 mm) travel

  • Front wheel/tire: 21 in spoked, 90/90‑21 (tube‑type)

  • Rear wheel/tire: 17 in spoked, 140/70‑17 (tube‑type)

  • Brakes: Single 300 mm front disc, single 230 mm rear disc, 2‑piston calipers, switchable ABS (both wheels)

  • Seat height: 34.3 in (870 mm)

  • Ground clearance: 7.3 in (185 mm)

  • Wheelbase: 61.2 in (1,555 mm)

  • Fuel capacity: 4.2 gal (16.0 L)

  • Curb weight (claimed): 428–430 lb (194–195 kg), depending on market

  • MSRP (US): $6,599 base / $7,499 SE at launch
clean_summary: Published in: Bikes Is the 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 right for you? Verdict The 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 is a lively, mid-size adventure twin that shines on pavement and fast gravel, offering more power and excitement than most of its direct rivals while staying approachable for a wide range of riders. It falls short for hardcore off-road or long-distance luggage-heavy travel, but as a do-it-all commuter, weekend tourer, and light ADV explorer, it hits a very sweet spot. Kawasaki revived the KLE name with the 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 , a modern 500‑class adventure twin that delivers class‑leading power, engaging street performance, and enough off‑road capability to finally tackle those gravel roads you usually ride past. Slotting into the booming mid-size adventure motorcycle segment, it targets riders who want one bike to commute during the week, tour on weekends, and still have funt when the pavement ends. To see what this new adventure machine is capable of, we rode the KLE500 in Utah for sweeping curves, deep sand, and relentless headwinds. Slotting neatly between small‑capacity singles and big‑bore ADV behemoths, the KLE500 aims to be the one bike that can commute during the week, munch miles on the weekend, and still feel at home when the pavement ends. It’s entering a seriously crowded space—up against bikes like the KTM 390 Adventure R , CFMOTO Ibex 450 , Moto Morini Vettore 450 , and Royal Enfield Himalayan 450 —but Kawasaki is clearly betting that a fun engine, sensible ergonomics, attractive price tag, and a recognizable badge will win over a lot of riders. Is it enough to dominate the arena? • Some History: Bringing Back The KLE Name The KLE badge has been around longer than many riders realize. Kawasaki first used it in the early 1990s on the original KLE500 , a twin-cylinder all-rounder sold mainly in Europe which used the venerable Ninja 500 motor we came to love in North America. That bike was part commuter, part light tourer and part dual-sport which made it a practical, slightly quirky machine. Although it was criticized for being underpowered with weak brakes, the old KLE never made huge headlines, but it earned respect from riders who prioritized comfort and versatility more than outright performance. When the first generation KLE500 disappeared from the lineup in the 2007, Kawasaki’s adventure duties split between the road-focused Versys (called the KLE 650 ) series and the single-cylinder KLR 650 . Despite it's demise, some of the KLE500's fairing styling lived on with the long overdue KLR 650 redesign in 2008. That left a gap: there was no mid-size, twin-cylinder adventure model that followed the KLE affordable, fun, do-it-all formula. Fast forward to 2026 and that gap is finally filled. The new KLE500 is a modern reboot that borrows Kawasaki’s latest 451 cc parallel-twin platform and wraps it in a package clearly designed for mixed-surface riding. Where the original KLE was ahead of its time, the new model arrives right as demand for affordable and manageable ADVs is rising. • What’s New on the 2026 KLE500: Engine, Chassis And Key Features At the heart of the new KLE500 is Kawasaki’s 451 cc liquid-cooled parallel twin, shared with the Ninja 500 , but is tuned for broader midrange while keeping that screaming top-end output of 51hp from the factory. The numbers won’t scare anyone at around 51 hp and 31.7 lb-ft of torque, but it's still about 10hp more than it's nearest class competitor. Plus, it delivers that power in a fairly linear manner all the way up to it's 10,000RPM redline. It’s smooth, eager to rev, and most importantly, very predictable which makes this an incredibly fun bike on asphalt.  Even better, thanks to the Ninja 500's success, there's already good aftermarket analysis and support to make notable improvements in the new KLE's output, that will honestly bury it's modern competition in terms of output. For riders coming from sport or naked bikes, the engine feels familiar. It likes to spin up and doesn’t surprise you with a sudden torque spike that can send you sideways on loose surfaces. As a lower speed rider, it's easy to miss the every-day friendly mid-range torque as it does require riders to keep it higher in the rev range. That said, it will lug out of low-rpm, missed-shift turns without much complaint, but this is definitely a top-end biased engine more oriented towards "spirited riding."  I found it fun and exciting to have a motor to push hard all day, but doesn't have so much power I worry about breaking traction or speed limits too quickly. Can the 451cc motor take it? For sure, it's bred for this and is accompanied by a healthy sized radiator to manage the heat. For riders stepping down from bigger bikes, it offers enough power to make highway passes and fast backroad runs enjoyable, without the “I’m one mistake away from disaster” feeling some big twins carry in the dirt. We were able to pull up to 70mph in 3rd gear going up hill into the wind on an entrance ramp. This leaves plenty of room for relaxed highway cruising thanks to the six-speed gearbox with a slipper clutch which provides a light lever pull and tidies up aggressive downshifts. That’s a comfort bonus in traffic and a confidence booster on loose descents or when you misjudge a corner and bang down a gear too many. Regarding vibrations, I personally felt it was very smooth all the way through according to my butt, feet and hands "vibrometer." I also use mirror vibration to gauge this, and there were very little vibrations through the KLE's stylish, but small mirrors. Over the past 20 years of moto-journalism, I've found every rider is sensitive to vibrations in their own way so while I'd say it wasn't bad, it's normal to have different opinions on this and encourage riders to get some first-hand experience on the bike. On the chassis side, Kawasaki has made all the right moves for credibility in the adventure space beginning with a new, rigid trellis frame that weighs only 40 lbs of it's 430lb wet weight. The overall build quality of the bike is solid and simple. All of this rolls on a 21-inch front and 17-inch rear spoked wheel combo, shod with IRC GP-410 Trail Winner ADV-oriented tires. While the IRC tires were OK and no testers had any major problems during the test, I think upgrading the stock tries with your favorite set of skins would be one of my first upgrades. One picking point of the community is the 17" rear wheel with a slightly odd 140/70-17 size. It does make the rear wheel a little "small" for  dual-sport applications, but for most riders who plane to spend a vast majority of their time on smoother surfaces, its not a problem. Actually, there's one benefit to it, which is it help keep the saddle height down to a relatively low 34". I was able to completely flatfoot the new KLE500 with a 32" inseam also partly thanks to its narrow tank profile.  While you could remedy this by installing a higher profile tire, or 18" rim, we don't think it will be necessary for most riders except that moving to a 140/80-17 gets you a much wider selection of adventure tires. This leads us to another hot topic of the new KLE having "only" 7.3 inches of ground clearance which was largely overblown by the internet. Our testing route took us through some sandy single track with plenty of ruts whoops and washouts. For the most part, the clearance wasn't a problem except for the most aggressive riders who like to catch air. The real off-road limitation on the KLE500 isn't so much the ground clearance, but the suspension, which we'll talk about next. Suspension is handled by a 43 mm upside-down cartridge fork up front and a preload-adjustable shock at the rear, with enough travel (~8 inches front and rear) to take on rough backroads and moderate double-track without too much drama. While many riders in the group were perfectly able to handle some of the more difficult single track at speed, the stock, largely unadjustable suspension is by-far the 2026 model's biggest hinderance in terms of off-road performance. Fuel capacity sits at about 4.2 gallons, which puts real-world range in the “a couple hundred miles if you’re sensible” neighborhood. We were seeing over 50mpg with aggressive riding in windy conditions, so expect mid 50's under normal riding. It’s not a huge tank, but for most riders mixing commuting, day rides, and occasional multi-day trips, it's more than adequate. • Ergonomics And Equipment The standard KLE500 ABS offers the same engine and suspension as the higher-spec KLE500 SE ABS for an additional $900. The trade-off is a more nervous feel off-road and a buzzier feel on longer highway stints. The Royal Enfield Himalayan 450 is arguably closest in spirit to the KLE500 . It’s friendly, torquey down low, with very balanced suspension, but it’s slower and more relaxed on the road. Riders who value plodding reliability and classic charm will love it; riders coming off sportbikes may find it a bigger step down in performance than they’d like. The CFMOTO Ibex 450 ( full review here ) and Moto Morini Vettore 450 share a similar platform and lean heavily into value and feature lists. They offer strong spec-for-price value and are rapidly improving in reputation. Some key features these models have but are not found on the KLE500 are adjustable suspension, 18" rear wheel, an exgtra inch of clearance and tubeless rims. But in many markets they still can’t match Kawasaki’s dealer network, long-term parts support and, possibly, resale value. The KLE500’s niche is clear: it’s the smooth, peppy twin that feels natural to street and sport riders, wrapped in an adventure package that looks good and doesn’t overwhelm newcomers or downsizers. It won’t out-tech the KTM or undercut the CFMOTO on price, but it delivers a polished, well-balanced experience backed by a brand and dealer network many riders already trust. • Final Thoughts: Who Sould Buy the KLE500 The KLE500 is a bike with a very clear audience in mind, and if you fit into either of these groups, it should be on your test-ride list. First, it’s a natural fit for new riders coming from sport or street backgrounds. If you’ve owned a streetbike and are now curious about gravel roads, BDR routes, or light adventure travel, the KLE500 is an easy choice. The engine speaks a language you already know, while the chassis gently introduces you to the looser, more forgiving rhythm of mixed surface riding. Whether you want to tour, commute or explore rough roads, the KLE500 has plenty to offer. Second, it’s an excellent option for experienced riders looking to downsize from bigger, heavier ADV or touring rigs and want to stick with a familiar brand. Maybe your current bike is starting to feel like too much in the garage, in the parking lot, or on the trail – but you’re not ready to give up twin-cylinder smoothness or the ability to cover highway miles at a decent clip. The KLE500 lets you shed weight and complexity while keeping enough performance to still enjoy the ride. In some ways, it's like a mini- Tenere 700 without the foot-stomping, tire romping torque. Don't let the initial social media, doom scrolling, echo chamber pundits get you down about the new KLE500 . It's a great bike for the right rider. I think what we have is an incongruency between how the bike is presented and what the bike is meant to be by design. This is not a new problem and it's obvious to the community. Making the KLE500 look like a rally bike, then using the word "Rally" in the tagline messaging would naturally make someone think this is an aggressive, off road, race-ready bike—which it's not, at least in current stock form. I think it can be a serious off-road contender, and maybe one of the best in class, with aftermarket upgrades to suspension and tires. There's plenty of options on the market for taller enduro and dual-sport bikes. We wear different shoes for different occasions and bikes are no different. As such, we want to evaluate bikes for what they are intended to be according to price, features and application. This is about having options in the market to grow the community, not insisting every bike should satisfy all our demands, and be affordable to boot. For the Kawasaki "Team Green" fans, we expect more than a few die-hard KLR650 riders will check out the KLE500 as a modern lightweight replacement. It would be an upgrade and replacement for the Versys 300 , which is a bike we love, but was unfortunately not promoted much after the initial launch. The KLE500 may ultimately replace these aging platforms, and with some thoughtful future upgrades, be a top mid-sized ADV, do-it-all bike for a wide range to real-world riders. Pricing will vary by market, but Kawasaki positions the KLE500 competitively in the mid-size adventure space. The standard KLE500 ABS comes in at a price that undercuts many European rivals while still offering a modern engine and a practical spec sheet. In the USA it comes in three colorways: Metallic Carbon Gray/Ebony for the base model or Metallic Bluish Green and Pearl Blizzard White for the SE version. The SE ABS version costs $900 more but bundles in upgrades that most riders would likely add anyway, making it arguably the better value if your budget allows. The final value proposition is simple: a smooth, flexible and forgiving twin that can do anything most riders would ask of it. The 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 , and many of its midsize competitors, are not about bragging rights. It’s about having a bike you actually want to ride and be part of your daily life. Whether that's going to work, the store, or that gravel backroad you've had to cruise by too many times the new KLE is a great option. For new riders stepping into adventure and current riders sizing down from the big stuff, that might be the most compelling spec of all. We're excited to see the new KLE500 fill out what's an amazing selection of mid-size adventure bikes, never before seen in North America, if not he world. There's never been a better time to get into riding, not only for the adventure, but as a companion you can enjoy everyday. No matter what you want to do, Kawasaki's new KLE500 can take you there and back again in a sharp-looking, fun, friendly and affordable package. You just have to get on, and ride! For more information, visit your local Kawasaki dealer or visit: Kawasaki.com Photos by: KawasakiUSA/ Justin Coffey and Carl Parker • Pros Fun, high output, bulletproof engine with slipper clutch Solid build quality with rigid frame Comfortable seat height and off-road ergonomics Edgy rally styling and riding geometry Easy to disable ABS • Cons Unfamiliar stock tires and odd rear tire size Suspension tuned more for on-road riding than off-road Non-adjustable suspension Ground clearance limited for agressive or tecnical riding Carl Parker is the publisher of ADVMoto ( Adventure Motorcycle Magazine ), where he has helped grow a niche adventure/dual‑sport title into an internationally distributed print and digital brand over the past 16 years. A lifelong photographer, artist, rider and storyteller, Carl is known for championing small‑displacement adventures and expanding the ADV community through accessible, real‑world travel content for everyday people. 2026 Kawasaki KLE500 – Key Specs Engine: 451 cc liquid‑cooled DOHC parallel twin, 8 valves Power: 51 hp @ 10,000 rpm Torque: 31.7 lb‑ft @ 7,500 rpm Transmission: 6‑speed, assist & slipper clutch, chain final drive Frame: Trellis, high‑tensile steel Front suspension: 43 mm inverted fork, approx. 8.3 in (210 mm) travel Rear suspension: Uni‑Trak monoshock, preload‑adjustable, approx. 7.7 in (200 mm) travel Front wheel/tire: 21 in spoked, 90/90‑21 (tube‑type) Rear wheel/tire: 17 in spoked, 140/70‑17 (tube‑type) Brakes: Single 300 mm front disc, single 230 mm rear disc, 2‑piston calipers, switchable ABS (both wheels) Seat height: 34.3 in (870 mm) Ground clearance: 7.3 in (185 mm) Wheelbase: 61.2 in (1,555 mm) Fuel capacity: 4.2 gal (16.0 L) Curb weight (claimed): 428–430 lb (194–195 kg), depending on market MSRP (US): $6,599 base / $7,499 SE at launch

title: Continental TKC 80 Gen 2 Review
summary:
Published in: Gear

Continental TKC 80 Gen 2 Review: Still the ADV Tire Benchmark?

The Continental TKC 80 has long been considered the benchmark for adventure motorcycle tires, trusted by riders tackling everything from highways to backcountry trails. With the release of the TKC 80 Gen 2, Continental aims to refine that legacy with improved grip, stability, and longevity. But does it still hold its crown in an increasingly competitive ADV tire market? We reviewed a new set on pavement and dirt to see how it compares to old and new competitors.

My history with the TKC 80 goes back decades. In 2008, I bought my first adventure bike, a brand-new BMW R1200 GS Adventure, which rolled off the showroom floor wearing a fresh set of Continental TKC 80s. From that moment on, nearly every ADV bike I’ve owned has run TKC 80s—that is, until a little SNAFU a few years back left fans scrambling. If you know, you know.

Since then, I’ve tested a wide range of tires from brands like Motoz, Bridgestone, and Pirelli, to name a few. Plenty of great options exist—but I always found myself comparing them to the TKC 80. For me, it’s long been the gold standard 50/50 adventure tire in the ADV space. For context, I’m not a pro racer, nor am I an especially aggressive rider. I’m just a guy who’s been riding a long time and prefers the kinds of roads most people avoid. I’ve logged tens of thousands of miles through my involvement with Backcountry Discovery Routes (BDR) over the past 14 years—many of them on TKC 80s.  Let’s dig in.

• What’s New About the TKC80 Gen 2? 

Before we even get to how the tire feels, Continental has a pretty long list of updates. The TKC 80 Gen 2 gets a 17% larger contact patch to boost grip and stability, along with a new offset tread block pattern that’s designed to bite better in softer terrain. Block spacing has been revised to keep traction more consistent, and the compound has been tweaked to balance on-road compliance with off-road grip and better wet traction.

Underneath it all, the carcass construction has been improved for better stability and comfort, and there’s a redesigned bead and breaker structure that’s meant to hold air more reliably and smooth out the ride.

They’ve also addressed irregular wear and managed to shave a bit off the road noise, which is always welcome on a knobby. In short: more grip, better feel, and longer life—on paper, at least.

• On-Road Performance

We kicked things off at the BMW Performance Center— in Greer, South Carolina. With 135 acres of dirt, gravel, mud, sand, and a couple of excellent tarmac tracks, it’s about as close to a dual-sport tire testing playground as you can get. We had our pick of bikes: the BMW F 900 GS, BMW R 1300 GSA, or the BMW R 1300 GS. I went with the latter—the go-to bike in my personal garage.

It began in 40-degree weather on brand-new, right-out-of-the-mold tires. Immediately throwing the bikes into tight, low-speed cone drills reminiscent of police riding courses the tires never felt sketchy in turns. Confidence came easy—though I’ll admit, I was occasionally more focused on not obliterating cones than analyzing grip.

As speeds picked up on the track, we worked through swerves, slaloms, and hard braking drills before getting some open laps. This is where the Gen 2 really stood out. For a proper knobby, the road manners are improved over the previous generation and I noticed a reduction of road noise and vibrations when at speed on highways.

Lean angles felt natural, braking was predictable, and stability at speed was noticeably improved. I’m no track junkie, but I had zero hesitation pushing hard—and there may even have been some boot-scraping involved.

The ride back on the highway gave me one last takeaway. The ride on the road was not vibey at all - a common complaint about dual-sport tires on the road. The Gen 2 was quiet and smooth for a 50/50 tire. Thanks to the subtle changes in the block pattern and spacing.The Gen 2 is quieter than the previous version. For a street-legal knobby, that matters more than you might think if your trips regularly see more than an hour or two of asphalt surfiing.

• Off-Road Performance

In the afternoon, things got messy—in the best way possible. We hit everything: dirt, deep gravel, mud, ruts, and the infamous sand pit. The new TKC 80s handled it all with ease. The tread cleared well, even in that greasy South Carolina orange clay, and never felt overwhelmed.

Where I really noticed the improvement was on edge grip. The revised tread pattern has much more agressive shoulder lugs than the previous generation. This not only make them look the business, but provides more bite when getting deep into it.  The tire felt planted and predictable through tight trees, off-camber sections, and rutted climbs.

Traction was consistent, and feedback was solid—exactly what you want when things get technical.

• Real-World Testing

Day two blended pavement and dirt with a ride into North Georgia’s twisties before dropping into the Chattahoochee BDR-X. This is where a 50/50 tire proves itself.

The Gen 2 delivered the same confidence I’ve always associated with the TKC 80—the kind that lets you commit without second-guessing. One highlight (or, depending on your perspective, a test) was a long water crossing over the Tallulah River, complete with slick rocks.

I'm happy to report there was no drama. No casualties or major mishaps, just smooth crossings all around. Whether thanks to the tires, the experienced riders, or both, the new TKC 80 Gen 2 showed no signs of compromise.

• Final Thoughts

In my search for a new go-to big block knobby over the last few years, I landed on the Motoz Tractionator tires. After running them on quite a few BDR expeditions I have to say the Motoz are neck and neck the TKC-80 Gen 2. The TKC might edge out the Motoz on the road but I'd be hard pressed to rate one better than the others in the dirt.

So, did Continental deliver? In my opinion—yes. The TKC 80 Gen 2 manages to feel like two tires in one: composed and confidence-inspiring on pavement, yet aggressive and capable in the dirt. That’s the holy grail for a BDR-ready tire. But, it’s not cheap with an MSRP between $268 and $399. Those not ready to make the jump, the original TKC 80 is still available at a lower price point.

We should see the second gen TKC80s in popular sizes showing up for sale around May. Then in August, some other size like 110 and 120 fronts..

After several hundred miles of rigorous on and off-road testing, it's safe to say if your adventures regularly blur the line between asphalt and backcountry, Continental's new TKC 80 Gen 2 deserves serious consideration. The host of improvements comes at a cost, but if it's within your budget, you're a fan of the original big blocks and want one of the most capable tires around, defintely give it a try. 

For more info, visit: Continental Tires Motorcycle Product Website

Photo Credits: Continental Tires, Lori Cannon and Neale Bayly

Tim James -  TIm is a lifelong motorcyclist, and spent nearly a decade and a half riding the backroads and trails of America, helping BDR grow into a cornerstone of the ADV community. Board President and Creative Director, Backcountry Discovery Routes
